title: The First Holiday Season After a Loss
summary: The first holidays after losing someone often bring grief back sharply — there's no single right way to move through them.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: The First Holiday Season After a Loss | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: The first holidays after losing someone often bring grief back sharply — there's no single right way to move through them.
whatYouNeedToKnow: It's completely reasonable to change traditions, skip the holiday entirely, or keep everything the same.
Grief often intensifies around anniversaries and holidays, and that's expected.
Planning ahead, even loosely, tends to ease the day itself.

paragraphs: Some families find comfort in keeping traditions exactly the same, honoring the person's memory; others need to change things significantly to make the day bearable. Both are valid, and there's no obligation to decide based on what others expect.

It's common for grief to intensify in the days leading up to a holiday, even more than on the day itself — anticipating this, rather than being surprised by it, can make the actual day feel a little more manageable.

nextSteps: Decide deliberately, as a family, whether to keep, adapt, or skip traditions this year.
Expect grief to intensify beforehand, and plan light days around that.
Consider a small, intentional way to acknowledge the person if that feels right.
disclaimer: However you choose to move through this season is valid. If it feels overwhelming, a grief counselor or support group can offer additional support.
